# THE 4TH GORDON COLLEGE DISTINGUISHED ALUMNI SERVICE AWARD: Call for Nomination 2025
[Image: THE 4TH GORDON COLLEGE DISTINGUISHED ALUMNI SERVICE AWARD: Call for Nomination 2025]
[Alumni](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/alumni/)
**Monday, August 4, 2025**
**GENERAL INFORMATION AND GUIDELINES**
Pursuant to Gordon College Alumni Association, Inc. (GCAAI) Board Resolution No. 4, series of 2022, the GCAAI establishes the DISTINGUISHED ALUMNI SERVICE AWARD (DASA) to honor Gordon College graduates who have excelled both in their professional and personal careers and who have served as an example to other alumni, students, and the community, both locally and globally. Starting this year, the GCAAI Board of Trustees will recognize the exemplary service and distinguished achievements that brought honor to the Institution and have made significant contributions to society. The GC-DASA recipients will exemplify the core values that the Institution uphold since its establishment: Exceptional Character, Remarkable Excellence, And Unselfish Service.  
The GC-DASA shall be the highest honor bestowed by the Alumni Association and will be an event to be celebrated annually as part of the GC Founding Anniversary and/or GC Alumni Homecoming.  
**GENERAL GUIDELINES:**
1. Only graduates of Gordon College (or GC, formerly Olongapo City Colleges) are eligible for the GC-DASA. The nominee shall be a member of the Gordon College Alumni Association, Inc. as confirmed by its Board.
2. Any GC graduate, faculty member, or stakeholders may make nominations for the GC-DASA. Self–  
   nominations are accepted. The nominee shall accomplish the Official Nomination Form to be provided by the GCAAI. No limitation will be placed on the total number of nominations accepted for consideration.
3. The nominee shall have no pending administrative or criminal case upon nomination. He/she shall exhibit a high degree of character & integrity.
4. Only one recipient per college/program will be awarded. In case the college/program will have no  
   nominees/recipients, the awarding ceremony will still commence. Likewise, the GCAAI Board of Trustees can directly, without prejudice to the respective college, nominate and evaluate for such an award though the college/program have already a candidate.
5. Only living candidates are considered. In the event of the death of a candidate between notification of the award and the award ceremony, the award may be given posthumously with the permission of the family and the GCAAI. In the event that the awardee declines to accept the award and/or fails to attend the awarding ceremonies, the GCAAI reserves the right to withdraw the award.
6. A GC graduate sitting as a member of the selection committee or a member of the GCAAI Board of Trustees is not eligible to receive the award. This individual is eligible for nomination after a one-year waiting period from the date he/she steps down from his/her role.
7. Recipients are only eligible for one GC-DASA in a lifetime.
8. The recipients will receive a plaque or certificate of recognition and will be posted on the official FB page of the GCAAI. It will announce the annual recipients through various methods and honor each recipient with the appropriate recognition.
9. If for any reason, the GCAAI is not able to conduct the Awards as planned (including due to tampering, unauthorized intervention, fraud, technical difficulties, pandemic, or any other causes beyond the control of the organizer which impact the administration, security, fairness, integrity or proper conduct of the Awards), then they reserve the right in their sole discretion to make any reasonable variation to the Awarding Ceremony.
10. The Nomination Committee and/or GCAAI Board of Trustees may disqualify any nominee that they  
    reasonably determine has breached any of the terms and conditions or otherwise engaged in unlawful or other improper conduct that may bring the Awards into disrepute or jeopardize the fair and proper conduct of the Awards.
11. Other guidelines may be promulgated as decided by the GCAAI Board of Trustees.
**NOMINATION COMMITTEE:**  
The Nomination Committee (NC) may be organized by the Dean/OIC in every college and/or the GCAAI. The committee must be composed of at least three (3) members from their respective colleges. The total count of committee members should be an odd number. The chairperson of the NC shall be the Dean or its duly authorized representative and for the GCAAI, any member can initiate such nomination. No member of the NC must be related within the second degree of consanguinity or affinity to any of the nominees/candidates for awards. If the Dean/OIC is related within the second degree of consanguinity or affinity to any of the candidates for awards, the Dean/OIC must inhibit him/herself from participating in the process. The approval shall come from the person next in rank.  
The NC shall:
1. Abide by the criteria set forth by the GCAAI.
2. For nominees endorsed by the College, it shall issue a certificate of authenticity to all the documents that will be submitted to the GCAAI.
3. Deliberate on the qualifications of the nominees for the awards based on the criteria and the documents submitted.
4. Recommend to the GCAAI Board of Trustees or its Alumni Association President the result of nomination for approval.
5. Recommend to the GCAAI Board of Trustees or its Alumni Association President the resolution on any related issue that may arise from the results of the awards.
6. Ensure that the guidelines stipulated herein are followed.
**NOMINATION AND SELECTION PROCESS:**
1. Complete the online/hard copy Nomination Form.
2. Nominators will receive an auto-generated copy via email of what they submitted, i.e., the nomination form including any attachments. In any case, it is not available, the NC may determine what means of notification it will conduct to the nominators to confirm the submission of the nomination form and its attachments.
3. Nominees and nominators will be contacted by the NC/GCAAI within three days of receipt of the  
   nomination to acknowledge receipt.
4. A nominee may be asked to clarify or submit further information and evidence to assist the NC/GCAAI in assessing the nomination at any stage of the selection process.
5. The NC/GCAAI may also seek verification of the nominee’s achievements from their current or previous employer or other relevant individual or organization representative. By being nominated for this Award, the nominees are giving consent to the NC/GCAAI to contact these individuals to verify nominations.
6. Nominations will be reviewed and shortlisted by the NC/GCAAI and nominees who have not been  
   shortlisted will be notified. The GC-DASA candidates will be asked to submit a brief profile for this purpose. The candidate shall garner a total score of 60 points to be included in the shortlist. If a nominee cannot meet the required points, he/she is automatically excluded from the shortlist.
7. Every member of the NC will recommend/endorse the final candidate to the GCAAI President and then, the GCAAI Board of Trustees, sitting as Awards Selection Committee, will issue a confirmation through a Board Resolution. The report on the results of the NC shall be signed by all members of the committee and certified by the chairperson.
8. In any case that such protest will be filed during the confirmation of the final candidates, the final decision on the protest shall be decided by the GCAAI Board of Trustees. The GCAAI Board of Trustees’ decision is final on all matters relating to the nomination process and the selection of award recipients, and they are not required to provide feedback as to why a nomination was unsuccessful.

# ONLINE Adding/Dropping of Classes
[Image: ONLINE Adding/Dropping of Classes]
**Monday, January 19, 2026**
**ANNOUNCEMENT:**
**ONLINE Adding/Dropping of Classes**
*Second Semester Academic Year 2025-2026*
**January 19-30, 2026**
[Image: ☑️]
Step 1
Log-on to the student portal ([https://gordoncollegeccs.edu.ph/gces/student](https://gordoncollegeccs.edu.ph/gces/student?fbclid=IwZXh0bgNhZW0CMTAAYnJpZBExZWpzSlY3MHlQdVJWaXA0bnNydGMGYXBwX2lkEDIyMjAzOTE3ODgyMDA4OTIAAR5DCtJJZeE0IeMkAlOWYkAjlJ5HTa_uyuxL5M64lTq3dzJieHkPXJaQ0YalBg_aem_XfOZPmWrrx4p7jcYpDLqDg))
Click Add/Drop Request Button under Menu Items.
[Image: ☑️]
Step 2
To request for Dropping of classes, under Current Schedule section, click Drop button.
To request for adding of classes, under Add Subjects section, type the detail of the class (you can search by class code, subject or by description) on the search box. Click Add button of the desired class.
[Image: ☑️]
Step 3
Your selected subject(s) will appear under List of Subjects to Add/Drop section. Repeat step 2 to add or drop more classes.

[Image: ☑️]
Step 4
Your request will be evaluated by your program coordinator and will be verified by the college registrar.
Regularly visit the GCES portal for request updates.

# 𝐈𝐬𝐚𝐧𝐠 𝐩𝐫𝐨𝐠𝐫𝐞𝐬𝐢𝐛𝐨 𝐚𝐭 𝐈𝐧𝐤𝐥𝐮𝐬𝐢𝐛𝐨𝐧𝐠 𝐩𝐚𝐦𝐮𝐦𝐮𝐧𝐨, 𝐛𝐢𝐧𝐢𝐠𝐲𝐚𝐧 𝐧𝐠 𝐛𝐚𝐠𝐨𝐧𝐠 𝐩𝐞𝐫𝐬𝐩𝐞𝐤𝐭𝐢𝐛𝐨
[Image: 𝐈𝐬𝐚𝐧𝐠 𝐩𝐫𝐨𝐠𝐫𝐞𝐬𝐢𝐛𝐨 𝐚𝐭 𝐈𝐧𝐤𝐥𝐮𝐬𝐢𝐛𝐨𝐧𝐠 𝐩𝐚𝐦𝐮𝐦𝐮𝐧𝐨, 𝐛𝐢𝐧𝐢𝐠𝐲𝐚𝐧 𝐧𝐠 𝐛𝐚𝐠𝐨𝐧𝐠 𝐩𝐞𝐫𝐬𝐩𝐞𝐤𝐭𝐢𝐛𝐨]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Friday, July 18, 2025**
Pinaglaanan ng panibagong pananaw ang mga kasalukuyang student leader ng Dalubhasaan Gordon patungkol sa paksa ng pamumuno bilang lider sa katatapos na Capacity Building and Personality Development Program for Student Organizations 2025 na ginanap sa Function 1, SMX Olongapo City Convention Center.
Ang naturang seminar na may temang: “Leadership Rooted in Purpose: Strengthening Self, Serving Others, Shaping Tomorrow” ay dinaluhan ng dalawang resource speaker mula Central Luzon State University (CLSU) na sina Dr. Irene G. Bustos, CLSU Student Affairs and Services Dean at ni Ms. Joan B. Pelagio, CLSU Paruparong Bukid Adviser.
Ang nabanggit na talakayan ay pinasimulan ni Dr. Bustos sa kanyang presentasyon tungkol sa kung paano nga ba ang tamang pamumuno ng isang lider sa kanyang nasasakupan at mga kalidad na dapat niyang tinataglay. Binigyang pansin din ni Dr. Bustos kung paano gawing reyalidad ang bisyon ng isang lider.
Sa kabilang banda, binuksan naman ni Ms. Joan B. Pelagio, CLSU Paruparong Bukid Adviser, ang diskusyong hinggil sa pagiging gender-responsive at pagbuo ng isang inklusibong kapaligiran at sa loob ng isang institusyon.
Ayon kay Ms. Pelagio, sa pamamagitan ng gender-responsiveness at inklusibong pakikitungo, mas makakabuo tayo ng pagkakaisa, respeto, at ligtas kapaligiran.
“Nag-start kami with few so with support and respect na hindi kami hinahayaan kung hindi sinusuportahan kami na gawin kung ano yung mga activities na kailangan, ‘yun yung isa sa nagmo-motivate sa group o sa org para ituloy yung advocacy.” Saad ni Ms. Pelagio ukol sa lagay ng Paruparong Bukid sa kanilang institusyon.
Si Ms. Pelagio ang tagapayo ng Paruparong Bukid, isang organisasyong nabuo sa isang obserbasyon at pagsasaliksik nina Dr. Bustos patungkol sa homophobia at transphobia na siyang naging pasimundan ng MAYARI na organisayon naman ng LGBTQIA+ ng Dalubhasaang Gordon.
Sa kabuuan ng nasabing seminar, “If you have passion for change, keep that passion fired, ika nga, [and] lighted, and pursue your vision and make it a reality. Be aware of everything that happens within and outside yourself. And kung anuman ang mga challenges, mga issues, problems ‘no parte lahat ito ng pagiging leader kaya hold on and keep the fire burning — yung passion mo to lead and to make change for the betterment of your studentry, the community, and siyempre the nation as a whole.” Ani Dr. Bustos sa mga kasalukuyang student leader at nagnanais na maging lider sa hinaharap.

# GC Color Run Kicks Off 27th Founding Anniversary
[Image: GC Color Run Kicks Off 27th Founding Anniversary]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Sunday, February 22, 2026**
The annual GC FUNdation RUN marked the start of Gordon College’s 27th Founding Anniversary, held on February 22, 2026, at the East Tapinac Oval Track.
The event gathered members of the Gordon College Faculty Association (GCFAS), administrative staff, students, alumni, and community participants under the pre-foundation day celebration motif, “Color Run.”
The program commenced with the assembly of participants, followed by an opening ceremony that featured welcome messages from Dr. Katherine Yap and Dr. Imelda D.P. Soriano. A Zumba warm-up session, led by administrators, faculty members, and students from the Bachelor of Physical Education (BPED), energized attendees ahead of the race.
The run officially started at 6:30 AM as participants filled the track dressed in white shirts and athletic attire, ready to be splashed with vibrant colored powder and water.
More than a display of color and movement, this year’s FUNdation RUN is a colorful display of the institution’s commitment to promoting wellness, unity, and community relations while marking the upcoming foundation celebration with lively and memorable moments. | via Sam Andrea R. # 𝐏𝐚𝐠𝐥𝐢𝐥𝐢𝐩𝐚𝐭 𝐧𝐠 𝐤𝐚𝐩𝐚𝐧𝐠𝐲𝐚𝐫𝐢𝐡𝐚𝐧 𝐬𝐚 𝐛𝐚𝐠𝐨𝐧𝐠 𝐭𝐞𝐫𝐦𝐢𝐧𝐨, 𝐩𝐨𝐫𝐦𝐚𝐥 𝐧𝐚𝐧𝐠 𝐧𝐚𝐢𝐬𝐚𝐤𝐚𝐭𝐮𝐩𝐚𝐫𝐚𝐧
[Image: 𝐏𝐚𝐠𝐥𝐢𝐥𝐢𝐩𝐚𝐭 𝐧𝐠 𝐤𝐚𝐩𝐚𝐧𝐠𝐲𝐚𝐫𝐢𝐡𝐚𝐧 𝐬𝐚 𝐛𝐚𝐠𝐨𝐧𝐠 𝐭𝐞𝐫𝐦𝐢𝐧𝐨, 𝐩𝐨𝐫𝐦𝐚𝐥 𝐧𝐚𝐧𝐠 𝐧𝐚𝐢𝐬𝐚𝐤𝐚𝐭𝐮𝐩𝐚𝐫𝐚𝐧]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Friday, July 18, 2025**
GORDON COLLEGE — Isinalin na ang mga posisyon at kapangyarihan sa mga bagong opisyales, gayundin ang pagpapakilala sa mga bagong kakatawan sa mga GCians sa katatapos na Supreme Student Council Turnover Ceremony na ginanap sa PE Hall nitong ika-14 ng Hulyo.
Nasaksihan din ng mga lider-estudyante mula sa iba’t ibang organisasyon sa loob ng institusyon ang nabanggit na seremonya.
Bilang panimula, binigyang diin ng noo’y SSC Chairperson Frances Daphne Mobo ang seremonya sa paglalahad ng Budget at Accomplishment Reports ng kanyang termino.
Samantala, inamin ni Mobo na nabigo nilang tuparin ang ilang mga pangakong proyekto para sa mga mag-aaral.
“Failures make us better individuals. Leadership teaches you how to be accountable [for] your own actions and how to accept constructive criticism. To our student leaders present here today, do remember that leadership is not about positions or titles because those are temporary. Leadership is about passion and dedication,” aniya.
Bukod dito, binigyan ng pagkakataon ang bagong termino upang talakayin at bigyang linaw ang kanilang mga inihandang proyekto at aktibidades sa harap ng kanilang mga kapwa lider-estudyante.
Sumatutal, mayroong 12 na proyekto at 17 na aktibidades na gustong ilunsad ang naturang termino sa pangunguna ni ngayo’y SSC Chairperson Julier Samonte. Ilan dito ay ang GCLAMP 2.0: Enhancing Access in the GCLAMP, CODE 200: Tech Industry Partnership Found, BIN-novation: Classroom for a Cleaner Future para sa kanilang proyekto, samantala ay inahain naman ang Student First Starter Packs: College Life Unboxed, Think Green: Green Revolutions for GCians, Beyond the Myths: Understanding HIV/AIDS & Prevention para sa kanilang mga aktibidades.
Ngunit ang lahat ng kanilang programa ay pawang mga panukala pa lamang at posibleng mayroong ibang hindi mapayagang mailunsad, paglilinaw ng bagong konseho.
Bilang karagdagan sa mga pangako ni Samonte, bagong kinatawan ng konseho, nais niya at ng kaniyang konseho na palawakin ang komunikasyon at transparency sa pagsasagawa ng regular student consultations, open forums, at online suggestion platforms para marinig ang tunay na saloobin ng mga estudyante. Kasama rin dito ang paglabas ng general plan of actions, budget breakdowns, at accomplishment reports upang mas mapanatili ang tiwala ng bawat estudyante sa konseho.
“Pangatlo, mas makabuluhang events para sa lahat. Gagawin nating inclusive at meaningful ang bawat SSC-led event. Hindi lang ito basta kasiyahan dahil gagawa rin po tayo ng mga aktibidad na magtataguyod ng leadership, advocacy, at student development.”
“Pang apat, pagpapalakas ng bawat student organization sa bawat departamento. Bilang inyong chairperson, sisiguraduhin kong makakaramdam ng buong suporta ang bawat departamental organization,” dagdag pa niya.
Paalala naman ng bagong SSC Chairperson na ang kaniyang pagkapanalo noong nakaraang eleksyon ay hindi lamang tagumpay ng isang tao kundi tagumpay rin ng bawat estudyante na naniniwala na pwedeng marinig, makilahok, at maging bahagi ng pagbabago.

# New Supreme Student Council proclaimed
[Image: New Supreme Student Council proclaimed]
[OSWS](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/osws/) [SSC](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/ssc/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Tuesday, May 6, 2025**
FRONTLINES | 𝑰𝒏 𝒕𝒉𝒆 𝒄𝒂𝒍𝒍 𝒕𝒐 𝒕𝒉𝒊𝒏𝒌 𝒘𝒊𝒔𝒆 𝒂𝒏𝒅 𝒗𝒐𝒕𝒆 𝒔𝒎𝒂𝒓𝒕, 𝒄𝒂𝒏 𝒂𝒏𝒚𝒐𝒏𝒆 𝒓𝒆𝒂𝒍𝒍𝒚 𝒔𝒂𝒚, ‘𝑰 𝒕𝒉𝒐𝒖𝒈𝒉𝒕 𝒘𝒊𝒔𝒆𝒍𝒚 𝒂𝒏𝒅 𝒗𝒐𝒕𝒆𝒅 𝒔𝒎𝒂𝒓𝒕’?
As the election results unfold, the following were proclaimed the new Supreme Student Council for A.Y. 2025-2026:
𝐂𝐡𝐚𝐢𝐫𝐦𝐚𝐧: 𝐒𝐚𝐦𝐨𝐧𝐭𝐞, 𝐉𝐮𝐥𝐢𝐞𝐫
𝐕𝐢𝐜𝐞 𝐂𝐡𝐚𝐢𝐫𝐦𝐚𝐧: 𝐏𝐫𝐚𝐝𝐚𝐬, 𝐃𝐡𝐚𝐧𝐚 𝐃𝐞𝐥
𝐒𝐞𝐜𝐫𝐞𝐭𝐚𝐫𝐲: 𝐒𝐚𝐛𝐮𝐬𝐚𝐩, 𝐀𝐧𝐭𝐡𝐨𝐧𝐲 𝐀.
𝐓𝐫𝐞𝐚𝐬𝐮𝐫𝐞𝐫: 𝐃𝐚𝐯𝐢𝐝, 𝐏𝐫𝐢𝐧𝐜𝐞
𝐀𝐮𝐝𝐢𝐭𝐨𝐫: 𝐃𝐨𝐦𝐢𝐧𝐠𝐨, 𝐏𝐡𝐨𝐞𝐛𝐞 𝐀𝐧𝐧𝐞 𝐒.
𝐏𝐈𝐎: 𝐄𝐬𝐭𝐫𝐞𝐥𝐥𝐚, 𝐉𝐨𝐡𝐧 𝐑𝐞𝐢 𝐄.
𝐂𝐀𝐇𝐒 𝐑𝐞𝐩𝐫𝐞𝐬𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐯𝐞: 𝐔𝐦𝐚𝐝𝐡𝐚𝐲, 𝐑𝐨𝐧𝐞𝐫 𝐓𝐫𝐨𝐬
𝐂𝐁𝐀 𝐑𝐞𝐩𝐫𝐞𝐬𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐯𝐞: 𝐂𝐚𝐬𝐚𝐦𝐨𝐫𝐢𝐧, 𝐍𝐚𝐝𝐢𝐧𝐞 𝐀𝐧𝐧 𝐃.
𝐂𝐂𝐒 𝐑𝐞𝐩𝐫𝐞𝐬𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐯𝐞: 𝐀𝐥𝐛𝐞𝐫𝐭𝐨, 𝐒𝐞𝐚𝐧 𝐃𝐚𝐯𝐢𝐝
𝐂𝐄𝐀𝐒 𝐑𝐞𝐩𝐫𝐞𝐬𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐯𝐞: 𝐄𝐛𝐚𝐥𝐨, 𝐋𝐲𝐧𝐚𝐫𝐝 𝐉𝐚𝐲 𝐏.
𝐂𝐇𝐓𝐌 𝐑𝐞𝐩𝐫𝐞𝐬𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐯𝐞: 𝐘𝐨𝐧𝐞𝐲𝐚𝐦𝐚, 𝐌𝐚𝐫𝐲 𝐘𝐨𝐬𝐡𝐢𝐤𝐨 𝐃.
As the students answer the call to vote wisely—which did not and will not end there—echoes collective raps to this new set of leaders about the accountability and responsibility of their respective positions.
Nothing much was asked by the students and for the students.

# LIMC History
Gordon College Library started in 1999 when the former Olongapo City Colleges, now Gordon College, put up a curricular program for Computer Studies and Business Management and Accountancy. With only a few books and mostly computer subjects in hand, Mrs. Remedios Sapuan, who was then a Utility worker, took charge of the library operation with the assistance of the Administrative Staff.
The Library moved four times to different locations in the building and its fourth was in the former KHG Hall. When the library was first opened, books were in the cabinet with glass doors and were locked. The library was using the close shelf system, where stacks cannot be directly accessed by users. The students should approach the assigned staff when borrowing books and it was then the bookshelf was opened to get the book needed.
In 2002, Thelma Ramos, a clerk aid of the Assessor’s Office, was transferred to the College Library to assist Mrs. Sapuan in the library operation. According to Mrs. Sapuan, a certain instructor, in the person of Mr. Sydney Maglalang, was temporarily assigned to the library. Services of the staff in the City Library were borrowed in the processing of books acquired for the College Library. In June 2003, the first College Librarian was hired, Ms. Maria Corazon D. Maneja as a casual position. Later she was promoted to a permanent position. The problem was by her was the book organization, the right classification of books, classification tools (DDC) Dewey Decimal Classification System and there were no catalog cards and cabinets. Some of the books were infested by termites which were already problems before but were not treated completely. Books were thenr eorganized, classified, and cataloged and were assigned new numbers using DD Classification tools that were borrowed from the Ci4ty Library. The circulation counter and grills dividing the books stacks and reading area were removed. Shelves were distributed and fastened on all library walls to make the books accessible to the users and to widen the reading area. Heavily termite-infested books were removed and deleted from the records. New books were formed. Newspapers were compiled with the date labeled and put on display. In 2006, books from Australia were received by the library through the Rotary Club of Olongapo. Books were found to be outdated and mostly for High School and Elementary level students. In the same year, another book donation was received from Asia Foundations, Inc. Books became very useful to patrons because of their general content. In March 2007, Ms. Betty Fielder, a friend of Mrs. Dely Aristorenas, former College Administrator, donated books and magazines for use by HRM students and faculty. Two air conditioners were installed to replace the former electric fans for ventilation and besides its small space, library patrons flocked the library especially nursing students who occupied the most number of enrollees. The library had then 8,361 volumes of books as of October 2007.
In the summer of 2010, the librarian during one of the EXECOM (ExecutiveCommittee) meetings, suggested removing over duplicated copies of books to provide spaces for new arrivals. Enough copies from weeded books were retained. 2,958 books were discarded and returned to GSO (Government Supply Office) but the latter suggested just donating them to the City Library.
In 2012, Gordon College's 13th Founding Anniversary, a Give-a-Book Project was launched and spearheaded by the College Library to collect tertiary level books from students, faculty, alumni, and friends of Gordon College. As of this date, May 6, 2013, we have collected 182 book donations.
On August 7, 2012, when the first knee-deep flood entered the College Library, books located on the first two-row level shelves were partially damaged but were restored. But on September 23, 2013, during a chest-deep heavy flood of Olongapo City, almost 2/3 of the library materials were soaked in the mud and water and devastated and discarded. Furniture which was made of light materials, files, and newly acquired books was included, but other computers were restored. Due to the kindness of City Mayor Rolen C. Paulino, he approved a 1 million budget for the purchase of new general education books, professional books, and journals. The first set of books was delivered in December 2013. Another era was opened for Gordon College Library for its transfer to the new building funded and built by SM in 2015-2016. The official transfer began on June 24, 2016, until July of the same year. The new and much wider library which is located on the fourth floor was opened to students on August 1, 2016, the start of the first semester, the Academic Year 2016- 2017.
Mr. Marlon A. Atencio recommended to the Executive Council the change of the library name into “Library and Instructional Media Center” for the reason that the library is now having a media center that supports the educational needs of the library and it was approved by the Executive Council spearheaded by the College President, Dr. Imelda DP. Soriano last May 31, 2018.

# 10 HEIs sa Central Luzon, pasok sa Times Higher Education Impact Rankings 2025
[Image: 10 HEIs sa Central Luzon, pasok sa Times Higher Education Impact Rankings 2025]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Monday, June 23, 2025**
Muling pinatunayan ng Central Luzon ang galing nito sa larangan ng edukasyon matapos mapasama ang 10 pampublikong Higher Education Institutions (HEIs) sa Times Higher Education (THE) Impact Ranking 2025.
Batay sa social media post ng Commission on Higher Education (CHED) Regional Office III, kabilang sa prestihiyosong listahan ang mga sumusunod na institusyon:
1. Central Luzon State University (801-1000)
2. Tarlac Agricultural University (1001-1500)
3. Bataan Peninsula State University (1501+)
4. Bulacan State University (1501+)
5. Don Honorio Ventura State University (1501+)
6. Nueva Ecija State University of Science and Technology (1501+)
7. Tarlac State University (1501+)
8. Gordon College (1501+)
9. Mabalacat City College (1501+)
10. City College of San Fernando, Pampanga (1501+)
Ang THE Impact Rankings ay sumusukat sa kontribusyon ng mga kolehiyo at unibersidad sa pag-abot ng United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), gaya ng dekalidad na edukasyon, gender equality, climate action, at iba pa.
Layunin nitong kilalanin at ipagdiwang ang mga institusyong aktibong nakikibahagi sa pagsusulong ng mas maayos at mas inklusibong mundo.
Ngayong taon, higit 2,000 universities mula sa 125 na bansa at teritoryo ang lumahok sa ranking. Sa bilang na ito, nasa 121 na institusyon sa Pilipinas ang kinilala, patunay ng lumlawak na global recognition pagdating sa kalidad ng edukasyon sa bansa.
Courtesy of CLTV36, click [here](https://cltv36.tv/10-heis-sa-central-luzon-pasok-sa-times-higher-education-impact-rankings-2025/?fbclid=IwY2xjawLhlMJleHRuA2FlbQIxMQABHjuiWKrpyze2X0ey_w9YqcxzFYqj-vVsA6VgMEYf5A0ksmU46n6TdywcL5ki_aem_Cu5pDIot_WbL-AJyV822ug) to read the full article.

The College of Education, Arts, and Sciences (CEAS) Mentors brought home the championship for Men’s Futsal against the College of Hospitality and Tourism Management (CHTM) Sharks with a score of 1-0.

# Beauty Meets Sportsmanship: Pislaan and Pradas Crowned as Mr. and Ms. Sportsfest 2025
[Image: Beauty Meets Sportsmanship: Pislaan and Pradas Crowned as Mr. and Ms. Sportsfest 2025]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Monday, September 15, 2025**
GORDON COLLEGE, OLONGAPO CITY— Cheers filled the East Tapinac Oval Grounds as the perfect blend of charm and athletic spirit came to life with representatives from various colleges showcasing their poise and sportsmanship, on the recently concluded Mr. and Ms. Sportsfest 2025 on Monday, September 15.
Kick-starting off the event with a bang, representatives wowed the crowd with their charisma and creative display of athletic-themed fashion for the Best in Sportswear award, which went to Mr. Chester Pislaan and Ms. Bhavjeet Kaur L. Singh from the College of Education, Arts and Sciences (CEAS) Mentors.
But the night didn’t end there—the battle for the ultimate title pushed the candidates to bring out their best poise, personality, and confidence. As the event drew to a close, the stage erupted in cheers and anticipation as the overall winners were finally announced.
After an intense showdown, Ms. Dhana Del C. Pradas from the College of Business and Accountancy (CBA) Wizards and Mr. Chester Pislaan from the College of Education, Arts and Sciences (CEAS) emerged as Mr. and Ms. Sportsfest—proving that talent, wit, and stage presence shine brightest when matched with determination and sportsmanship.
Furthermore, hot on their heels, the 1st Runner-Up titles went to Mr. Marc Anthony Acula (CBA) Wizards and Ms. Bhavjeet Kaur L. Singh (CEAS) Mentors, whose outstanding performances made them strong contenders throughout the competition.
Meanwhile, Mr. Krizztan Vic A. Dominico and Ms. Ron Eriele Joi P. Agustin from College of Allied Health Studies (CAHS) Tigers secured the 2nd Runner-Up spot, earning admiration for their dynamic partnership.
In 3rd place, Mr. Charles De Leon from College of Computer Studies (CCS) Phoenix and Ms. Alexandra Macky Alisbo from College of Hospitality and Tourism Management (CHTM) claimed their rightful spot. At the same time, Mr. Jack Francis P. Meran (CHTM) Sharks and Ms. Janna Lyn Rolls, CCS Phoenix, completed the roster as the 4th place finishers. | via Love Lorainne Venus Acedo/The FOREFRONT

# Clean As You Go & Bring Your Own Trash Policy
[Image: Clean As You Go & Bring Your Own Trash Policy]
**Friday, August 8, 2025**
Instructions for Gordon College Students: Clean As You Go & Bring Your Own Trash Policy
Purpose:
To foster a clean and respectful campus environment while promoting sustainability through personal responsibility for waste.
Before Class or Activity:
1. Prepare Your Materials:
– Bring Your Own Trash: Carry a small, reusable bag or container to collect any waste you generate during class or activities. This could be a sturdy tote, a lunch bag, or any suitable container.
– Plan for Snacks/Drinks: If you plan to consume food or beverages, pack them in a way that minimizes waste (e.g., reusable containers, bottles).
During Class or Activity:
2. Maintain Your Space:
– Keep Your Area Clean: Throughout the session, stay vigilant about your surroundings. Avoid leaving papers, wrappers, or any personal items strewn about.
– Immediate Clean-Up: If you create a mess (accidental spills, paper cuts, etc.), address it promptly. If cleaning supplies are available, use them to tidy up immediately.
3. Responsible Waste Management:
– Use Your Reusable Container: Whenever you have waste, place it in your reusable bag or container. Keep it nearby for easy access.
– Respect Shared Spaces: Be mindful of not just your space but also those around you. Dispose of any common area waste (e.g., napkins from community snacks) in your own trash.
After Class or Activity:
4. Final Cleanup:
– Inspect Your Area:
Before leaving, take a moment to ensure your seating area and any communal spaces (tables, chairs) are tidy.
– Empty Your Trash into Appropriate Bins: When you leave the classroom or activity, empty your reusable container at designated trash or recycling bins outside the classroom (if available). Take anything that belongs to you with you.
5. Dispose Responsibly:
– Recycle When Possible: If your waste is recyclable, place it in the recycling bin. Be sure to check the college’s recycling guidelines to ensure proper disposal.
Additional Responsibilities:
6. Encourage a Culture of Cleanliness:
– Remind Peers:If you notice a classmate leaving a mess, gently remind them or offer to help.
– Lead by Example: Demonstrating your commitment to the “Clean As You Go” policy can inspire your peers to do the same.
7. Feedback and Improvement:
– Participate in discussions or feedback sessions organized by the college to share your insights on how the Clean As You Go policy is working.
8. Engage with Initiatives:
– Stay updated on any college-led environmental initiatives or clean-up days, and participate actively when you can.
By adhering to these instructions, you contribute to creating a cleaner, more sustainable campus environment while developing valuable habits of personal responsibility and community stewardship. Thank you for doing your part!

# GCAAI Membership
#### Qualifications for Membership
A. The qualifications for membership to the Association are either:
1. Holder of academic degree from Gordon College (formerly Olongapo City Colleges)
2. Holder of secondary/basic education diploma from Gordon College-Senior High School
3. Holder of academic title or postgraduate diploma from Gordon College (formerly Olongapo City Colleges)
4. Not having been dismissed for misconduct/offenses or scholastic/academic deficiency which is automatically considered not a member of the Association.
B. Membership to the association may be bestowed upon the approval of the GC-AA Board of Trustees:
1. Holders of honorary degrees from the College;
2. Former or current members of the faculty and staff of the College who have rendered service to the College for at least ten (10) years.
C. Active members are those who have qualified as members under Section 1 and 2, and are either:
1. Members who have paid lifetime membership dues;
2. Members who have paid and are up-to-date in their annual membership dues;
3. Members who have participated in such other programs that sustain, develop, and promote the Association’s objectives as determined by the Board.
D. The Alumni Oath shall be recited by a member who signifies its willingness to be part of and reflect its allegiance to the Association. The Alumni Oath shall be administered by the President or its duly authorized representative in a ceremony, commencement and/or event. The Alumni Oath in its current form is as follows:
*I **(state your name)**,*  
*a graduate of Gordon College,*  
*hereby undertake to*  
*vigilantly upholds the ideals*  
*and shield the honor of my Alma Mater,*  
*and as a fulfilled member of the Gordon College Alumni Association, Inc.,*  
*will uphold its Constitution and By-Laws,*  
*to remain a true paragon towards excellence,*  
*committed to do justice to everyone*  
*in service to our country and our God.*  
*So help me, God!*

# LCUAA 2025 Welcomes Rainy Sunday Afternoon
[Image: LCUAA 2025 Welcomes Rainy Sunday Afternoon]
[LCUAA](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/lcuaa/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Sunday, October 19, 2025**
The eagerly anticipated week-long 14th LCUAA National Games officially kicked off today at the City of San Jose del Monte (CSJDM) Sports Complex.
Cheers and applause filled the venue as the opening ceremony began with a parade featuring all 27 participating colleges, each led by their institution’s gold medalists.
Following the parade was the introduction of the LCUAA 2025 representative officers, a doxology by the Sining Tanglawan ng San Jose del Monte Performing Group, the singing of the National Anthem and the San Jose del Monte city hymn, and an energetic dance performance by the San Joseño World Class Talents troupe.
City College of San Jose del Monte (CCSJDM) College President Dr. Roger S. Ambida delivered the opening remarks, warmly welcoming delegates to the institution.
Hon. Arturo B. Robes then gave the keynote address, encouraging athletes to build a brighter and more empowered future.
Additionally, Dr. Lora L. Yusi, CHED Region 3 Director IV, gave an inspiring speech, highlighting the significance of LCUAA 2025 as a testament to the growth of local universities: “We see scholars who dream larger than themselves.”
The competitive events officially begin tomorrow, October 20, 2025.
Stay tuned to the official Facebook page of The Forefront for updates on the 14th LCUAA National Games. | via Alyssa Marie Alcantara / The FOREFRONT

# MRP’s Reading Program
[Image: MRP’s Reading Program]
[Extension](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/extension/) [RDCES](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/rdces/) [Community Extension](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/community-extension/)
**Saturday, August 19, 2023**
The Reading Literacy Program expanded its scope to include learners within the DepEd system facing reading challenges, such as non-readers and those with difficulties in English and Filipino. The program held sessions on June 9 and 22, as well as August 12 and 19, attracting voluntary participation. Despite the short duration, significant improvements were observed in the majority of learners. The culminating activity, conducted simultaneously across 17 barangays in Olongapo City, was highly successful.
The program’s success was achieved through collaboration among student facilitators, immersion instructors, CESU coordinators, local barangay representatives, the mayor’s office, and the Rotary Club of Downtown Olongapo City. The Rotary Club played a crucial role in printing the booklets used during the sessions.

# Proper Usage of Glass Boards
[Image: Proper Usage of Glass Boards]
**Thursday, May 23, 2024**
GUIDELINES FOR THE USE OF GLASS BOARD INSTALLED IN THE CLASSROOMS
* Use White Board Marker only to write on it
* Always erase marker ink from the surface after each use to prevent stains and ghosting.
* Don’t use dirty eraser or cloth, it will start spread ink residue across the surface of the board.
* Glass board must be cleared of any markings before the next class starts.
* Avoid using tapes or adhesives on the glass surface. These materials may be difficult and cause permanent damage to the glass board.
* Don’t use your hands to wipe a glass board! Your skin carries natural oils that over time leave a build-up of oil residue on the board. This can eventually ruin the surface of the glass board.
* Do not use sharp objects to avoid scratching or damaging the board surface.
* If the glass board is accidentally broken, it must be replaced with the same brand within seven (7) days from the date it was broken. If the item with specific brand and specification is not available, it must be replaced with three (3) timex of the quantity or in quantity comparable to the price of the item. The brand and specification must be approved first by the concerned personnel before replacement.

# From Debits to Dominance: Pink Team (cPINKa) Credits the Ultimate Gamble Victory
[Image: From Debits to Dominance: Pink Team (cPINKa) Credits the Ultimate Gamble Victory]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Sunday, March 1, 2026**
Accountancy students of Gordon College gathered at East Tapinac Covered Court on February 25 for Junior Philippine Institute of Accountants (JPIA)’s Ultimate Gamble Team Building.
The participants collaborated, competed, and conquered through strategy-driven games that tested teamwork and unity.
Demy Anne M. Roallos headed the Green Team (Berde Makausad), Triskelly Lianne Cyril Cabildo led the Red Team (Pulang Ina), Felicity Dyle Rivas guided the Yellow Team (Bibeni Luca), Aran Benz C. Sardañas captained the Orange Team (Ilocos Empanada), Shakira Nicole B. Guintu steered the Pink Team (cPINKa – Certified Pink Accountants), Jan Lester M. Bitangcol led the Purple Team (Ooh-Lila), and Hannah Jhem M. Malit directed the Blue Team (BIR – Bluereau of Impeccable Recorders).
As the event began, each team performed their energetic yells and presented their flags to start the event with a blast. In the first game entitled “Caterpillar Game,” Green Team (Berde Makausad) won first place, Orange Team (Ilocos Empanada) gained second place, and Yellow Team (Bibeni Luca) took third place.
For the second game of “Pass the Balloon,” Red Team (Pulang Ina) achieved the first place, followed by the Pink Team (cPINKa), and lastly, the Yellow Team (Bibeni Luca). As the third game commenced, entitled “The Floor is Lava,” Pink Team (cPINKa) was hailed as the champion, Green Team (Berde Makausad) as the first runner-up, and the Orange Team (Ilocos Empanada) as the second runner-up.
Moreover, in the station games, the teams played in seven stations, namely Pass the Ball, Cut it Up, Wrap-tastic, Balloon Cup Relay, Rollercoaster Ball, T-Shirt Relay, and Calcu Relay. Yellow Team (Bibeni Luca) triumphed in first place, Purple Team (Ooh-Lila) succeeded in second place, while Pink Team (cPINKa) acquired third place.
After consolidating all performance entries like preparing a final financial statement, Pink Team (cPINKa) emerged as the overall champion with a total audited score of 185 points, followed by Green Team (Berde Makausad) with 180 points, Yellow Team (Bibeni Luca) with 175 points, Blue Team (BIR) and Red Team (Pulang Ina) who tied with 170 points, and lastly, Orange Team (Ilocos Empanada) and Purple Team (Ooh-Lila) who both garnered 150 points. Furthermore, Blue Team (BIR) won for the best yell, while Red Team (Pulang Ina) claimed the best flag award.
According to Pink (cPINKa) team leader, Shakira Nicole B. Guintu, their victory came from proper planning before the activities and going all-in on collaboration. “No’ng nag-activity po kami, laging may nagsasalita and nakikinig. Pwedeng higher batch, pwedeng lower batch, basta lahat naman po kami nakikipag-cooperate,” said Guintu.
Guintu also added that the team building reflected real-world challenges in accountancy, helping them to be patient and to remain calm under pressure. “May activities na parang paulit-ulit na kami pero nire-remind namin na kalmahan lang, na i-go lang sa flow namin, sa pace namin,” the team leader stated.
The activity ultimately reinforced that future accountants must not only master numbers but also embody the 3 C’s—Collaborate, Compete, and Conquer—skills essential in navigating real-world professional engagements and organizational challenges. | via Justine Stephanie A. Canlas/The FOREFRONT

# The celebration of arts and culture ignites a fiery PASE Community Concert this 27th Founding Anniversary
[Image: The celebration of arts and culture ignites a fiery PASE Community Concert this 27th Founding Anniversary]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, February 25, 2026**
The Performing Arts Student Ensemble (PASE) ignites a synergy of performances on the second day of the 27th Founding Anniversary on February 25, at East Tapinac Oval Grounds.
The first segment featured PASE Ballo’s contemporary dance performance to the song ‘Tala,’ touching the hearts of GCians with their moving steps.
Furthermore, PASE Voci’s chorale performance filled the air with harmonic voices and unity, creating an ethereal atmosphere through their cover of ‘Bagong Umaga’ by Bayang Barrios.
The third segment was a play by PASE Teatro, showcasing relatable acting that highlighted the reality of social media’s impact on people’s lives.
In addition, PASE Ballo returned for another performance of a cultural inspired dance filled with enthusiasm and energy.
Moreover, the fifth segment was a collaboration between all PASE ensembles — Cuerdas, Teatro, and Voci. Storytelling and music united on a narrative of Overseas Filipino Workers (OFWs) acted by Teatro and set to Voci and Cuerdas’s rendition of ‘Pinoy Ako’ by Bamboo.
Following that is a performance from Teatro’s newest troupe, the collectives. The four-member rap group sang Filipino hip-hop songs that captured the culture of Pinoy music history. They performed covers of ‘Venus’ by Ron Henely, ‘Ikaw lang’ by Kiyo, and ‘Upuan’ by Gloc 9, bringing back nostalgic vibes to many GCians.
Finally, the last segment featured a collaboration of Voci and Cuerdas. Following this is PASE Ballo’s dance performance, celebrating Olongapo’s culture and the jeepney tradition. The performance transitioned to Cuerdas and Voci, who performed a reggae song.
The event proudly celebrated National Arts Month this February, showcasing the diverse talents and artistry of GCians. Through various performances, the students highlighted their creativity and passion for the arts, demonstrating the vibrant cultural landscape of the institution. It was a testament to the rich artistic expression and talent that GCians have, making it a memorable celebration of arts and culture, leaving a lasting impression on the audiences at the 27th founding anniversary. | via Lei Ydah Lasala Ledesma / The FOREFRONT

# GC welcomes ALCUCOA Accreditation 2025
[Image: GC welcomes ALCUCOA Accreditation 2025]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, November 26, 2025**
The three-day accreditation kicks off as the Association of Local Colleges and Universities Commission on Accreditation (ALCUCOA) arrives today, November 26, at the Gordon College (GC) Main Bldg.
This year, the Bachelor of Science in Business Administration (BSBA) Major in Marketing Management vies for Level III Accreditation.
Furthermore, the Bachelor of Science in Accountancy (BSA), Bachelor in Elementary Education (BEEd), Bachelor of Early Childhood Education (BECEd), Bachelor of Secondary Education Major in Filipino (BSEd-Fil), and Bachelor of Secondary Education Major in Science (BSEd-Sci) are aiming for the Level II Accreditation.
Meanwhile, Bachelor of Culture and Arts Education (BCAEd) and Bachelor of Physical Education (BPEd) are now candidates for this year’s accreditation.
The accreditation 2025 is set from November 26-28, at the Function Hall, Gordon College Main Bldg. | via John Patrick Mateo/ The FOREFRONT

# GC honors DASArving alumni, highlights iconic “first-times” in 4th DASA
[Image: GC honors DASArving alumni, highlights iconic “first-times” in 4th DASA]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Tuesday, October 14, 2025**
SMX OLONGAPO CENTRAL— The 4th Distinguished Alumni Service Awards (DASA) 2025 gathered alumni who paved innovation and inspiration for Gordon College on October 11, held at SMX Olongapo Central.
Mr. Joseph Pusing, former Gordon College Alumni Association Inc. (GCAAI) President, proudly presented all the first-time projects that happened on the initiative of giving back to their alma mater: Alumni cards, Financial support for the student council and organizations, Cosme Y. Sering Library, and a fifty thousand cash donation for the College of Business and Accountancy (CBA) Department for the extension of rooms for accreditation.
“One of the highlights of our term is the donation of the alumni association’s newest student lounge, which was blessed on August 15, 2025. This will not be possible without the help and guidance of everyone,” he stated.
Also, Dr. Imelda DP. Soriano, the college president, exclaimed in her keynote speech that the world needs someone like them who wagers unselfish services in a world full of selfishness.
Furthermore, Ms. Rose Anne Cañeda Dumalo, alumna from the Bachelor of Science in Education – English (BSEd-English) Batch 2015, expressed in her speech that titles and recognition will not define us but the people that we serve along the way.
“Higit sa pagiging magaling ay ang pagiging mabuti. You may be the best in the field, but if you lack character, people will remember how you treated them more than how well you did your job,” she added.
Moreover, Ms. Ellinor T. Manaois-Yco, Bachelor of Science in Nursing (BSN) Batch 2011; Mr. Arben R. Cauilan, Bachelor of Science in Customs Administration (BSCA) Batch 2011; Nikki Antonette Chieng Carlos, Bachelor of Science in Tourism (BSTM); and Mr. Willy R. Antigo, Master of Arts in Education (MAEd) Batch 2019, were awarded with recognitions and plaques for their outstanding service and contributions.
In addition, Ms. Lovelyn P. Ceralde, DASA 2025 Overall Chairperson, extended her warm welcome and gratitude to the alumni, stakeholders, and those who have been bringing their best to provide a more quality education and experience inside the institution.
Finally, the program ended with an oath-taking for the newly elected GCAAI officers and board of trustees for the fiscal year 2025 – 2026. | via John Patrick Mateo/The FOREFRONT

# SUCs still face budget cuts in proposed 2026 national budget – Kabataan
[Image: SUCs still face budget cuts in proposed 2026 national budget – Kabataan]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Saturday, October 18, 2025**
“Sa ibang institusyon o unibersidad [man], ang matapyasan ng budget para sa sektor ng edukasyon ay isang paraan upang mawalan ng karapatan ang mga magaaral na magkaroon ng kalidad na edukasyon,” a 4th Year BACOMM Student exclaimed.
Twenty-five State Universities and Colleges (SUCs) are still facing budget cuts amounting to Php 6.88 billion after Congress rejected amendments by Kabataan Partylist in its second-to-last hearing of the proposed 2026 national budget.
In a Facebook Post, youth-led Kabataan partylist condemns the House of Representative’s rejection of proposed amendments to House Bill (HB) No. 4058 or the proposed 2026 national budget that would have alleviated deficits to SUCs.
“Mariing tinututulan ng Kabataan Partylist ang pahayag ng Kongreso na sapat na raw ang dagdag-badyet sa SUCs at ang pagpondo sa 2022-2025 Free Higher Education (FHE) deficiencies. Danas ng bawat Iskolar ng Bayan ang hirap na magpatuloy sa kanilang pag-aaral dahil sa kakulangan ng pondo at suporta mula sa gobyerno,” the youth-led party-list said in a Facebook post.
Furthermore, in a report by Krixia Subingsubing for Philippine Daily Inquirer, twenty-five SUCs will be affected by budget cuts amounting to Php 6.88 billion. Three of these institutions are from Central Luzon including Aurora State College of Technology, Bulacan Agricultural State College, Central Luzon State University in Nueva Ecija and Philippine Merchant Marine Academy in Zambales.
Meanwhile, a group of school presidents and student regents from these institutions, led by Kabataan Party-list, has since issued a unity statement calling for Congress to restore budget cuts from the proposed national budget next year, citing “chronic underfunding” of the country’s education system.
According to the group, budget cuts compel SUCs to restrict admissions and compensate with “internally generated income from business ventures [and on] direct fee collection from students”.
As of October 10, the House has approved the proposed Php 6.7 trillion 2026 budget bill for 2026 following amendments. The budget is scheduled for a final reading in Congress on October 13 before congressional break. | via Lance Isaac Leon/The FOREFRONT
Sources: Kabataan Partylist/via FACEBOOK, Krixia Subingsubing/PHILIPPINE DAILY INQUIRER

Gordon College (GC) secures third spot among top performing schools in the May 2025 Philippine Nurses Licensure Examination (PNLE), earning a rating of 98.29%, as 115 out of 117 examinees successfully made the cut.

# Stude orgs answer GC’s SOAE call

[Campus](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/campus/) [OSWS](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/osws/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, February 26, 2025**
Despite the short attendance of student organizations and delegates in the GC 4th Student Organization Awards of Excellence (SOAE), the awarding ceremony proceeded in citing its outstanding student organizations and student-leaders in service for A.Y. 2024-2025 at GC Function Hall, yesterday.
The 4th SOAE awarded, in no particular order, its recipients of:
Top 3 Outstanding Student Organization | Interactive Organization of Natural Sciences (IONS), League of Tourism Students of the Philippines (LTSP) – Gordon College Chapter, and Samahang Filipino (SamFil)
Top 3 Outstanding Student Organization Adviser | Jehan B. Doblado, Kristoffer Paul C. Obispo, and Mychale Williams
Top 3 Outstanding Community Extension Program | CHTM Student Council, Interactive Organization of Natural Sciences (IONS), and Samahan ng mga Mag-aaral sa Araling Panlipunan
Meanwhile, Nicolas Milton Ching earned the Servant Student Leader of the Year while Klaudtrev Hanrick Payumo merited Rookie Student Servant of the Year. Jannae Mae De Jesus received the Student Organization Presidential Award and Sophia Joleen Evangelista with Outstanding Student Extensionist Award.
And in no particular order, this year’s Top 10 Outstanding Student Leaders were:
Nicolas Milton Ching | SPECS
Jhoemari Corpus | IONS
Janna Mae De Jesus | SMAP
La Arnie Morales | CHTM SC
Dhana Eunice Papa | JFINEX
Kharl Christopher Nofies | SAMFIL
Lorenzo Don Cayabyab | LTSP
Ralf Vincent Paule | PSCAS
John Kyle Perez | SAMFIL
Jay Angelo Manalang | LTSP
Aeros Raphael Morales | LELLS
Lynard Jay Ebalo | IONS
Mark Louis Macachor | CHTM SC
Sophia Joleen Evangelista | SMAP
Moreover, LTSP-GC Adviser Mychale Williams won the Student Organization Adviser of the Year.
The CHTM Student Council came out once more as the Student Council of the Year among others while IONS, after bagging two other awards earlier, also flexed on its Spirit of Service Award plaque while LTSP nagged the Student Organization of the Year title.
This ceremony is still in line with the celebration of the 26th Founding Anniversary of the institution.

Huge congratulations to the College of Allied Health Studies (CAHS) for its outstanding performance in the recently conducted Nursing Licensure Examination (NLE) held on February 26–27, 2026. Gordon College has officially secured its spot as the TOP 4 PERFORMING SCHOOL with a remarkable institutional passing rate of 91.67%. This achievement is particularly exceptional as it more than doubles the National Passing Rate of 44.24%, proving once again that our educational standards are truly world-class. # CHTM Sharks sealed the set against CEAS Mentors in Men’s Singles
[Image: CHTM Sharks sealed the set against CEAS Mentors in Men’s Singles]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, September 17, 2025**
TRICKSHOT COVERED COURT—The College of Hospitality and Tourism Management (CHTM) Sharks bagged the gold again during the men’s badminton singles championship match, 21-6, defeating the College of Education, Arts and Sciences (CEAS) Mentors.
With his outstanding performance, Kryztofer Matias Basa of CHTM Sharks ended the finals with two straight sets, leaving no chance for his opponent to recover.
In an interview, Basa shared that he already expected the victory as he had once played with a stronger player before.
“‘Yu‘n pa yung ginamit ko na motivation para magchampion ulit yung CHTM,” he said when asked about being pressured to defend the previous win of CHTM last SIKLAB.
He also expressed his joy in playing with passion and humility. “Iniisip ko lang is maging humble, and…sportsmanship,” he added.
Moreover, the College of Computer Studies secured the bronze medal, while the College of Business and Accountancy and the College of Allied Health got the 4th and 5th place, respectively. | via Jeliza Tadena/The FOREFRONT

# Website Policy
### Website Committee
Gordon College (GC) Website Committee was formally established by Interoffice Memorandum No. 38 Re: Website Committee. The Interoffice Memorandum states the following; composition, duties, and responsibilities of Website Committee, Guidelines in Website Content Development and Management, and Procedure for Posting/Uploading Web Content.
### Composition
GC Website Committee will be composed of a Chairperson, Vice-Chairperson, Website Administrator, and Members.  
For the members, it will be the Head of the Information and Communication Services Unit (ICSU), one (1) faculty member from each department designated by the College Dean, and one (1) staff from each office/unit assigned by the Unit Head.
### Duties and Responsibilities of Website Committee
Based on Interoffice Memo No. 38, the following are the duties and responsibilities of the committee:
1. Develop and recommend web related policies and standards
2. Oversee the design and underlying technology
3. Ensure the website stays current with new technology/trends
4. Implement regular data collection methods
5. Use data collected to guide the framework and infrastructure of the college website
6. Create guiding principles to steer the framework and infrastructure decisions of the college website
7. Work with the Management Information System Unit (MISU) to set timelines for web engine upgrade and new website deployments.
8. Regularly assess the design of the website and make recommendations for improvement
9. Serve as the liaison between the College President and the different department regards content
10. Identify and advocate for web needs and services for the college community.
### Guidelines in Website Content Development and Management
1. Interoffice Memo No. 38, clearly states the guidelines to be observed by the committee in terms of the contents for the website.
2. All news, updates and all other content to be posted on the official website should have clearance from concerned departments/ units and approval from the College President.
3. The Website Committee is tasked to manage the postings on the official website in coordination with the Information and Communication Services Unit (ICSU). The committee shall formulate posting guidelines and ensure proper dissemination.
4. Queries, concerns or issues aired through the official website must be communicated to concerned departments through ICSU who shall also responsible for posting official responses to such queries, concerns or issues whether through online or offline as the situation warrants.
### Procedure for Posting/Uploading Web Content
The following are the guidelines stated in Interoffice Memo No. 38 in terms of posting/uploading content to the college website:
1. Requesting department should submit a copy of the post to ICSU for approval of the College President.
2. After approval, ICSU shall transmit the content to the Website Committee Chairperson and Web Administrator.
### Website Content Submission
The GC Website Committee is pleased to offer the opportunity to share knowledge and expertise by submitting content to the college website with the following guidelines:
* **Guiding Principles**  
  Gordon College is one of the institutions that promote excellent education in Region III and the country. As such, the committee would like to publish and post content, materials, and ideas that support the vision, mission, and goals of the college. The committee also encourages each individual from different colleges and departments to share their experience and insights as one of the people of Gordon College.
* **Style**  
  The Website committee encourages each and everyone to use the current best practices for web writing. These includes:
  + Begin with the conclusion by focusing on the main message and be clear on how you want the reader to feel and respond.
  + Add specific details, facts, figures, quotes, etc.
  + Submit at least one (1) graphs, graphics, or photos that are copyright free with a size of;
    - 1280 pixels by 720 pixels for Hero Banners (Congratulatory Posters)
    - 1200 pixels by 630 pixels for News, Announcement and Notice
    - 250 pixels by 250 pixels for Website Banner
    - 250 pixels by 100 pixels for Logo (Rectangle)
    - 100 pixels by 100 pixels for Logo (Square)
  + Graphs, graphics or photos should be in .jpeg, .png or .svg format
  + Use subheadings that reflect the content and captures the interests of readers
  + Use listed points with ten (10) words or less per item
  + Search the web for other pro tips on best practices
* **Publishing**  
  The committee does not guarantee that it will post your submissions automatically. All published contents shall be well-guided and must receive the approval of concerned departments, as stated on Interoffice Memorandum No. 38.It does guarantee, however, that the article will be read and explore whether or not it is a good fit for the college and the readership. In reviewing submissions, the committee is looking for content that is necessary and relevant to the needs of the stakeholders, especially with the faculty and students. Articles reflecting the inspiring, powerful, responsible, productive, and connected nature of the academe are the ones that we are looking for.
* **Copyright**  
  The committee will only post copyright-free materials submitted by the author and will not pay for articles posted on the website. The college reserves the right to use the submitted article or any part of it depending on the committee’s discretion.
* **Response Time**  
  The goal of the committee is to respond to the requestor as quickly as possible. Depending on the situation, the committee will do its best to respond to each submission in 1 to 3 days.
* **Requestor**  
  The following groups and/or individuals are allowed to submit content for posting:
  + Department Heads
  + Vice Presidents
  + Faculty Members and
  + Administrative Staff
  + Recognized Student Organizations
  + Alumni
### Specific Guidelines
To achieve uniformity and establish the visual identity of the college kindly follow the following in submitting your content;
* **Title and Author Information**  
  All submission must identify (to be written in the body of your message);
  + Topic
  + Title
  + Author’s Full name
  + Author’s Phone Number\*
  + Author’s Email Address\*
  + Department (if applicable)  
    *\* – needed for communication purposes*
* **Summary**  
  Provide a 20-30 words summary of the article that can be used for links.
* **Length of the Content**  
  Submit articles that are between 500-750 words. Please submit final drafts that have proofread, and grammar and spelling checked.  
  Do not include the graphics or photos within the document. It should be saved separately in the same folder where the file resides.
* **Formatting**  
  Send your document at **biron.neilmarc@gordoncollege.edu.ph** with the following format details:
  + Subject : Request for Posting (Website)
  + Body : Author’s Information
  + Topic : Event/ Celebration
  + Title : Sportsfest
  + Author’s Full name : Juan Dela Cruz
  + Author’s Phone Number : 607.7175
  + Author’s Email Address : juandelacruz@gmail.com
  + Department : College of Allied Health Studies
  + Summary of your article.
  + Attachment:
    - document file of your article
    - picture1.jpg\* / picture1.png\* / picture1.svg\*  
      *\* – you can select one particular image type*
* **Links**  
  Provide a list of referenced links in the text or at the end of the submitted article. The committee will review these links to determine if they are appropriate for the website and will determine if they will be included or not (especially for copyright acknowledgment).
* **Categorization of Submission**  
  Articles that will be posted in the website can be categorized into;
  + News
  + Announcement or Guidelines
  + Notice or Advisory
* **Tags**  
  This will be use to sub-categorized your submission;
  + CAHS *(College of Allied Health Studies)*
  + CCS *(College of Computer Studies)*
  + CBA *(College of Business and Accountancy)*
  + CEAS *(College of Education, Arts, and Sciences)*
  + CHTM *(College of Hospitality and Tourism Management)*
  + IGS (*Institute of Graduate Studies)*
  + OSWS*(Office of Student Welfare and Services)*
  + Campus
  + Alumni
  + Administration
  + Academics
  + Forefront
### Content Administration
All web content submitted must be approved prior to posting. The following individuals retain the right to edit or request changes:
* College President
* Website Administrator
* Head of ICSU
* Vice Presidents
* Requestor/Author of the Content
### Website Content Update/ Revision
Thou postings are checked for spelling, grammar and factual accuracy, there will be some instances that can result unwanted errors.
The GC Website Committee will allow update or revision of articles posted on the website for the following reasons;
* Missing or Duplicate Titles
* Misspelled names
* Overlooked misspelled words or grammatical errors
* Missing images or links
* Missing details or information
### Website Content Removal
The GC Website Committee reserves the right to remove any posted content that contains copyrighted materials which include, but not limited to: text, images, graphics, sound recordings, video recordings or software programs.

# GC 27th Founding Anniversary Schedule
[Image: GC 27th Founding Anniversary Schedule]
**Monday, February 23, 2026**
Gordon College proudly invites the entire GC community to take part in the 27th Founding Anniversary Celebration happening from February 24 to February 26.
Get ready for three days filled with meaningful activities, campus spirit, and memorable moments as we come together to celebrate our legacy, achievements, and the vibrant community that makes GC truly exceptional.
Mark your calendars and join us as we celebrate this milestone in Gordon College history.
See you, GCians!

# GC ALAB 2026: Connecting Job Seekers to Opportunities on GC’s 27th Anniversary
[Image: GC ALAB 2026: Connecting Job Seekers to Opportunities on GC’s 27th Anniversary]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, February 25, 2026**
In celebration of the 27th Founding Anniversary, the GC ALAB Job Fair was successfully conducted on February 25, 2026, at Ayala Malls Harbor Point – Activity Center from 9:00 AM to 3:00 PM.
The event was spearheaded by the Public Employment Service Office (PESO) in collaboration with Gordon College, particularly under the Community Extension Unit and the Office of Student Welfare and Services, as part of the institution’s commitment to serving its stakeholders by providing accessible employment opportunities.
A total of 30 participating companies joined the job fair, offering various career opportunities for job seekers and fresh graduates.
This meaningful activity was made possible through the partnership of Department of Labor and Employment (DOLE), SBMA Labor, Ayala Malls Harbor Point, JPMAP, SMAP, and the College of Business and Accountancy.
The GC ALAB Job Fair reflects Gordon College’s dedication to community extension and workforce development.

𝐏𝐑𝐎𝐅𝐄𝐒𝐒𝐈𝐎𝐍𝐀𝐋 𝐑𝐄𝐆𝐔𝐋𝐀𝐓𝐈𝐎𝐍 𝐂𝐎𝐌𝐌𝐈𝐒𝐒𝐈𝐎𝐍 (𝐏𝐑𝐂) 𝐎𝐍-𝐒𝐈𝐓𝐄 𝐒𝐄𝐑𝐕𝐈𝐂𝐄 𝐂𝐀𝐑𝐀𝐕𝐀𝐍
GORDON COLLEGE | OLONGAPO CITY, ZAMBALES
May 9, 2025
[Image: ✨]
It’s a wrap!
[Image: ✨]
The PRC On-Site Service Caravan at Gordon College was a resounding success, delivering a total of 549 services to Professionals in a single day. This initiative significantly improved access to essential PRC services within the local community—and is part of our ongoing commitment to support not only our Alumni but also to all Professionals.
Breakdown of Services Rendered:
• Renewal – 138
• Initial Registration – 60
• Duplicate – 11
• Certification – 170
• Authentication – 162
• Certificate of Special Order (COS) – 8
TOTAL – 549
A special acknowledgment to the dedicated PRC Committee for their exemplary service and leadership:
Ms. Elaine Perez Espela, Ms. Glenda B. Sapon (Team Leader), Ms. Joana Benigna Pabelonia, Mr. Joselito Dulay, and Mr. Angelo Pedro Hernandez.
We are also grateful to the student organizations for their invaluable support and active participation:
• College of Computer Studies Student Council (CCS SC)
• GCCCS Empowered League of Information Technology Education Students (ELITES)
Sincere appreciation goes to the VP for Institutional Planning, Development, and External Affairs, Dr. Kristin Joy A. Mendoza, for her instrumental role in the successful implementation of this initiative.
We would also like to express our heartfelt thanks to Mr. Joseph Angelo B. Pusing GCAAI President and Ms. Denise Lou Punzalan for spearheading this event, and to the Board of Trustees and Working Committee of the Gordon College Alumni Association Inc. (GCAAI) for their full support and commitment in making this initiative a success.
Special thanks to Dr. Imelda DP Soriano, College President, for her unwavering support in bringing services closer to the community.
This caravan is not just a service initiative—it’s our way of helping empower our alumni community as they continue their professional journeys.
Thank you to everyone who contributed to the success of the PRC On-Site Service Caravan. Together, we continue to broaden access to professional services and empower our community.

# CCS Phoenix Clinches Gold at SIKLAB 2025 E-Games
[Image: CCS Phoenix Clinches Gold at SIKLAB 2025 E-Games]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, September 17, 2025**
FUNCTION HALL, GORDON COLLEGE MAIN BLDG.—The Gordon College Main Building Function Hall buzzed with excitement as SIKLAB 2025 E-Games came to a spectacular close, featuring intense battles for gold, silver, and bronze.
In the Battle for Bronze, the College of Education, Arts, and Science (CEAS) Mentors defeated the College of Allied Health Studies (CAHS) Tigers, 22-8, securing third place and bringing pride to the CEAS community.
Meanwhile, the championship series between the CCS Phoenix and the CHTM Sharks was a nail-biter in a best-of-three format. CCS edged CHTM in Game 1, 21-20, followed by a 17-win in Game 2. In the final showdown, the Phoenix dominated, 12-1, clinching the gold medal with an overall 2-1 victory.
The CHTM Sharks earned the silver medal, demonstrating remarkable perseverance and strategy throughout the finals.
SIKLAB 2025 E-Games concluded as a remarkable showcase of determination, strategy, and camaraderie, crowning the CCS Phoenix as champions while bringing the excitement of competitive E-sports to Gordon College—and leaving fans eagerly anticipating next year’s edition. | via Frances Marie Farne/The FOREFRONT

# BLEPT 2025 Yields 200 New GC-Produced Professional Teachers
[Image: BLEPT 2025 Yields 200 New GC-Produced Professional Teachers]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Monday, December 22, 2025**
The College of Education, Arts, and Sciences (CEAS) beams with pride as it yields 39 Licensed Professional Teachers (LPTs) for the Elementary level and 161 new LPTs for the Secondary level at this year’s Board Licensure Examination for Professional Teachers (BLEPT)
Gordon College (GC) once again surpassed the national passing rates in the Board Licensure Examination for Professional Teachers (BLEPT) conducted on September 21 and November 30, 2025.
For the Elementary Level, 39 out of 49 Gordon College examinees passed the LEPT, resulting in a passing rate of 79.59% against a national passing rate of 51.04%.
Meanwhile, for the Secondary Level, 161 out of 177 Gordon College examinees passed the LEPT, posting an overall passing rate of 90.96%, with 50% of second-takers also passing.
Overall, 76,696 out of 122,528 examinees nationwide passed the September–November 2025 BLEPT, representing a national passing rate of 65.04% for the secondary level.
Notably, the Board Licensure Examination for Professional Teachers (BLEPT) was rescheduled in Regions I, II and Cordillera Administrative Region (CAR) due to inclement weather.
The examinations for the said regions were conducted on November 30. | via Lovely Doroin/The FOREFRONT

# CEAS Mentors, ibinida ang ginto sa Standard Category
[Image: CEAS Mentors, ibinida ang ginto sa Standard Category]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Monday, September 15, 2025**
GORDON COLLEGE, OLONGAPO CITY – Umangat ang kahusayan ng College of Education, Arts, and Sciences (CEAS) Mentors sa taunang Dance Sports Competition Standard Category, matapos matagumpay na hinataw ang dalawang katunggali sa entablado sa pamamagitan ng tatlong klasikong sayaw.
Sinubukan ang kanilang husay at indak sa Waltz na nagpakita ng pino at walang-kupas na galaw, sa Tango na umigting sa matinding ekspresyon, at Quickstep na sumubok sa bilis, koordinasyon, at tibay ng bawat pares.
Sa kabila ng dikit na laban, naging kapansin-pansin ang stage presence, chemistry, kontrolado ngunit makapangyarihang galaw ni Jieann Cahanding at Ivan Russel Bahala mula sa CEAS Mentors na nagpakita ng malinis na footwork hanggang sa makabagbag na emosyon sa entablado. Sinundan ito ng CHTM (College of Hospitality and Tourism Management) Sharks sa ikalawang pwesto at CAHS (College of Allied and Health Studies) Tigers sa ikatlong pwesto.
“Aside from being student athletes, of course. Kami kasi ng partner ko first time partner, yung adjustment doon na tinuturuan ko siya kasi hindi rin siya talaga dance sport athlete, pero yung nakakamangha sa kaniya ay madali siya turuan, saad ni Jieann ukol sa hinarap nilang problema bago tumungtong sa entablado.
“Yung struggle namin ay ‘yong time management medyo mahirap kasi tuwing hapon lang kami nakakapag practice,” dagdag pa niya.
“Sobrang saya po na nakakaiyak. As a first-timer malaking challenge sa akin yun at malaking achievement,” ayon kay Bahala patungkol sa naramdaman matapos sungkitin ang kampeyonato.
Sa pagtatapos ng patimpalak, hindi lamang talento sa pagsasayaw ang naipakita ng mga kalahok. Bawat kolehiyo ay nagbigay ng kani-kaniyang tatak—mula sa makukulay na costume, malinis na footwork, hanggang sa mga koreograpiyang may hatid na emosyon at kwentong Gcians. | via Lianne Malazarte/The FOREFRONT

# GC releases statement over alleged P19-million overpayment
[Image: GC releases statement over alleged P19-million overpayment]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Monday, January 5, 2026**
“The College wishes to clarify that funds received under the Free Higher Education Program are meticulously allocated and budgeted which undergoes strict audit by appropriate national government agencies. All collections approved were allocated transparently for the operations and development of the college,” Gordon College (GC) stated.
Following the publication of a Commission on Audit (COA) report cited by Bilyonaryo News Channel, GC released an official statement addressing the alleged P19-million overpayment under the Free Higher Education (FHE) Program.
The institution clarifies that all funds received were meticulously allocated and budgeted, emphasizing that all approved funds were used transparently for the institution’s operations and development.
According to the COA report, there are three (3) Local Universities and Colleges (LUCs) with ineligibilities and excessive fees due to loss in control mechanism of the Commission on Higher Education (CHED) Central Office and Unified Students Financial Assistance System for Tertiary Education (UniFAST) Billing Unit, amounting to more than P295-million.
The three institutions identified were the Tangub City Global College (TCGC), Pamantasan ng Cabuyao (PNC), and Gordon College (GC).
For GC, COA reported a total amount of P19,260,800 in questioned fees. The audit findings pointed to a mismatch between GC’s definition of a laboratory room and COA’s definition of an Audio-Visual Room (AVR).
Meanwhile, PNC listed an overpayment amounting to P55,818,690, which focuses on the leadership training, student partnerships, student publication and newsletters, and remedial programs under the Development category—being the highest recipient.
On the other hand, TCGC recorded the highest alleged overpayment at P220,706,895, with cultural fees accounting for the largest portion of the amount.
Furthermore, COA noted that this was not the first time CHED had been called out for overpayments under the FHE Program.
Audit observations from 2021 to 2022 already pointed to excess budgeting, while the 2024 report cited lapses in verification processes of the CHED Central Office and UniFAST Secretariat, resulting in the continued cycle of observations and disallowances since fiscal year 2022.
As part of its general recommendations, COA directed the three institutions to return the overpayments made by CHED, or to avail themselves of other appropriate modes of settlement of accounts in accordance with the existing laws and regulations.
In response, GC reiterated its commitment to accountability and transparency, emphasizing its willingness to cooperate with auditing authorities and comply with lawful processes. | via Mikhaela Sagun/The FOREFRONT

# World University Rankings 2026: GC Recognized as Reporter Institution
[Image: World University Rankings 2026: GC Recognized as Reporter Institution]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Saturday, October 18, 2025**
Gordon College has officially been granted Reporter Status in the recent Times Higher Education (THE) World University Rankings 2026, released last October 9.
The college is among six Higher Education Institutions (HEIs) in Central Luzon that have been granted this recognition, including:  
– Bulacan State University  
– Central Luzon State University  
– Nueva Ecija University of Science and Technology  
– Tarlac Agricultural University, and  
– City College of San Fernando Pampanga
The Times Higher Education annually releases their World University Rankings that measure the performance of universities in eighteen (18) performance indicators across five core pillars: teaching, research environment, research quality, international outlook, and industry. A reporter status is granted to HEIs who have submitted relevant data but did not meet eligibility criteria to receive a rank. Inclusion in the reporter status allows HEIs to receive feedback in their performance across the metrics to support future decision making.
Meanwhile, Ateneo de Manila University is the highest ranking university in the Philippines with a banded rank of 1001-1200, followed by the University of the Philippines (1201-1500), De la Salle University (1501+), Mapua University (1501+). Mindanao State University – Iligan Institute of Technology (1501+), and University of Santo Tomas (1501+). The University of Oxford remains to be the top global ranking university for the tenth consecutive year. | via Lance Isaac Leon/The FOREFRONT
Sources: Times Higher Education World University Rankings 2026 and CHED Regional Office III (via Facebook)

# WAY TO GO! GC on its 3-PEAT VICTORY
[Image: WAY TO GO! GC on its 3-PEAT VICTORY]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, October 29, 2025**
WAY TO GO! Congratulations to our College on its 3-PEAT VICTORY, emerging as CHAMPION at the Philippine Statistics Quiz – Provincial Elimination! The event was held on October 28, 2025, organized by the Philippine Statistics Authority (PSA) Zambales.
We are so proud of our students who are now Qualifiers for the Regional Competition on November 6, 2025, at the Orchid Garden Resort, Pampanga:
1. Andrei Fredeluces
2. Sophia Alaiza Esnaldo
3. Rachelle Andrea Rivera
This incredible achievement was guided by our dedicated coaches, Mr. St. Lawrence Abogadie and Mr. Ryan Justin Fruto.
Special thanks as well to our BSEd in Mathematics Program Chair, Dr. Katherine D. Yap, for her unwavering support and for representing the College on behalf of our College President, Dr. Imelda DP. Soriano.
